perturbation.original_text: Section 2.1 Transferred IP. (a) Acquired Company IP. For the avoidance of doubt, the Purchaser Group will acquire all Acquired Company IP by virtue of the acquisition by the Purchaser Group of the Acquired Company Shares pursuant to the terms of the Acquisition Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Section 2.1 Transferred IP. (a) Acquired Company IP. For the avoidance of doubt, the Purchaser Group may acquire some Acquired Company IP by virtue of the acquisition by the Purchaser Group of the Acquired Company Shares pursuant to the terms of the Acquisition Agreement, but this is not guaranteed and depends on further negotiations.
perturbation.explanation: The original text states that the Purchaser Group WILL acquire ALL Acquired Company IP, while the modified text states that the Purchaser Group MAY acquire SOME Acquired Company IP, but it is not guaranteed, which contradicts the initial, definitive statement. This in-text contradiction creates uncertainty regarding which IP is being transfered and creates ambiguity for the term "Acquired Company IP" and contradicts the text located in Section 3.1 Ownership, where it is mentioned which items of Assigned IP and Acquired Company IP is owned by each party.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Section 3.1 Ownership. With respect to each item of Assigned IP and Acquired Company IP, respectively, such item is owned by a Seller Party or an Acquired Company, respectively, and such Seller Party or Acquired Company, respectively, has marketable title to such item. Schedules 1.1(b) and 1.1(c) of the Seller IPA Disclosure Schedule collectively set forth a complete and accurate list of all registered, issued, or applied-for Intellectual Property that constitutes Acquired Company IP and Schedules 1.1(h), 1.1(l), and 1.1(n) of the Seller IPA Disclosure Schedule collectively set forth a complete and accurate list of all registered, issued, or applied-for Intellectual Property that constitutes Assigned IP (such Acquired Company IP and Assigned IP, collectively, the "Registered Intellectual Property"). For each listed item, each of Schedule 1.1(b), 1.1(c), 1.1(h), 1.1(l), and 1.1(n) of the Seller IPA Disclosure Schedule, as applicable, indicates, as applicable, each owner of each such item of Registered Intellectual Property, the jurisdictions in which each such item of Registered Intellectual Property has been issued or registered or in which any application for such issuance or registration has been filed, the registration or application number, and the application filing or registration dates thereof. Each such item of Registered Intellectual Property is subsisting and, to the Knowledge of the Seller, valid and enforceable (except with respect to applications for Intellectual Property). A Seller Party has sufficient right, title, and interest in and to the Licensed IP to grant the licenses granted under this IP Agreement with respect thereto.
perturbation.justification: The original text unequivocally states that the Purchaser Group "WILL acquire all Acquired Company IP." The changed text introduces uncertainty by stating the Purchaser Group "MAY acquire some Acquired Company IP, but this is not guaranteed." This directly contradicts the contradicted text, Section 3.1, which assumes definitive ownership of "each item of Assigned IP and Acquired Company IP" by the Seller Party or Acquired Company. The modified language undermines the assumption of complete acquisition, creating a contradiction concerning the ownership and title of the Acquired Company IP, thereby creating ambiguity where certainty was previously established. Schedules 1.1(b) and 1.1(c) of the Seller IPA Disclosure Schedule collectively set forth a complete and accurate list of all registered, issued, or applied-for Intellectual Property that constitutes Acquired Company IP. With the changed text there is no guarentee whether or not it will be owned by the seller or acquired company.

perturbation.original_text: Section 2.2 Licensed IP. (a) Patent License. Effective as of the Initial Closing Date, the Seller hereby grants (and will cause each other Seller Party to grant following each applicable Closing Date), to the Purchaser Licensees an irrevocable (except as expressly set forth herein), perpetual, non-sublicenseable (except as expressly set forth herein), fully paid-up, royalty-free, worldwide, non-transferable (except as expressly set forth herein), non-exclusive license, under the Licensed Patents:
perturbation.changed_text: Section 2.2 Licensed IP. (a) Patent License. Effective as of the Initial Closing Date, the Seller hereby grants (and will cause each other Seller Party to grant following each applicable Closing Date), to the Purchaser Licensees a revocable, temporary, sub-licenseable, subject-to-royalties, limited to North America, transferable, and exclusive license, under the Licensed Patents:
perturbation.explanation: The original text grants an irrevocable, perpetual, non-sublicenseable, fully paid-up, royalty-free, worldwide, non-transferable, non-exclusive license. The modified text directly contradicts these terms by making the license revocable, temporary, sub-licenseable, subject-to-royalties, limited to North America, transferable, and exclusive, creating an In-Text Contradiction, and uncertainty regarding the terms of the license. This perturbation contradicts with what is mentioned on Section 4.2 Irrevocability of Licenses, specifically with the mention of 'irrevocable licenses'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Section 4.2 Irrevocability of Licenses. Each of the parties acknowledges and agrees that the licenses granted hereunder (i) are irrevocable and (ii) may not be terminated for any reason (even in the event of a material breach), except that (a) with respect to Licensed IP that constitutes Third-Party Intellectual Property, solely as provided in Section 2.4(a), (b) with respect to a particular Patent licensed under this IP Agreement, the license granted to such Patent shall automatically terminate upon the expiration of the statutory term (including all extensions and renewals) of such Patent, and (c) Seller may terminate the license granted to the Purchaser Licensees pursuant to Section 2.2(c) (and no other provision of this IP Agreement), following the Initial Closing Date and upon prior written notice to Purchaser, in the event Purchaser materially breaches Section 2.2(c) and fails to cure such material breach within one hundred twenty (120) days after Purchaser's receipt of written notice from Seller (which such notice shall contain a reasonable description of such material breach and a statement of Seller's intent to terminate the license granted to the Purchaser Licensees pursuant to Section 2.2(c) if such material breach is not cured within such one hundred twenty (120) day period). Nothing herein shall preclude any party from seeking damages or other remedies at law or in equity (other than termination of this IP Agreement or any license to any Intellectual Property granted under this IP Agreement) for any breach hereof.
perturbation.justification: The modified text in Section 2.2(a) introduces multiple contradictions with Section 4.2, Irrevocability of Licenses. The original text grants an irrevocable license, while the changed text makes it revocable, directly conflicting with Section 4.2's assertion that the licenses are irrevocable. Further, the original license is perpetual whereas the new license is temporary which also directly contradicts Section 4.2 which states the licenses may not be terminated for any reason. The new license is subject to royalties, which would contradict the original license which was royalty-free. All of these changes introduce clear and impactful contradictions within the document, affecting the understanding and enforceability of the license terms.

perturbation.original_text: Section 2.3 Delivery. (a) Documentation. To the extent in the possession or under the control of any Seller Party, the Seller shall provide (and shall cause the other Seller Parties to provide) to the Purchaser: (i) promptly after the Initial Closing Date, complete and accurate copies of all the following that constitute Transferred IP: file histories and notes (where such notes are regarding, with respect to Transferred IP, actual or potential disclosure dates or prior art dates, standards-essential Patents, or license or covenants not to sue granted to any Person with respect to such Transferred IP) from the Seller Parties' docketing systems of the pending Patent applications and issued Patents (and invention disclosures, if any, for all such applications and Patents that any Seller Party is able to provide using Reasonable Efforts), pending Trademark applications and Trademark registrations, Copyright applications and Copyright registrations, and unpublished Patent applications;
perturbation.changed_text: Section 2.3 Delivery. (a) Documentation. To the extent in the possession or under the control of any Seller Party, the Seller may provide (and may cause the other Seller Parties to provide) to the Purchaser: (i) at their discretion after a period of 10 years after the Initial Closing Date, incomplete and potentially inaccurate copies of some of the following that constitute Transferred IP: only the most recent file histories and notes (where such notes are regarding, with respect to Transferred IP, covenants not to sue granted to any Person with respect to such Transferred IP) from the Seller Parties' docketing systems of the pending Trademark applications and Trademark registrations, Copyright applications and Copyright registrations, and published Patent applications;
perturbation.explanation: The original text specifies a clear obligation for the Seller to provide complete and accurate copies of documentation related to Transferred IP promptly after the Initial Closing Date. The modified text introduces ambiguity by changing 'shall provide' to 'may provide,' altering the timing to 'at their discretion after a period of 10 years', reducing the scope of delivery to just the most recent file history and reducing the type of IP Documentation to specific trademark and copyright applications, and it goes on to state that the documentation delivered might be incomplete and potentially inaccurate. These changes introduce an in-text contradiction by making the delivery of vital documents uncertain and also contradicts Section 6.5 Further Assurances.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Section 6.5 Further Assurances. Each of the parties agrees that from time to time, at the reasonable request and expense of the other party, it shall execute and deliver such other documents and take such other actions as the other party may reasonably request to effectuate the transactions contemplated by this IP Agreement (including any short form documentation evidencing the licenses granted by any Seller Party hereunder or other documentation to perfect or record the rights granted hereunder in the Transferred IP or Licensed IP in any jurisdiction throughout the world). The Seller acknowledges and agrees (including on behalf of the other Seller Parties) that the Purchaser or any of its Affiliates may record and perfect this IP Agreement or such documentation in any jurisdiction throughout the world, and the Seller shall (and shall cause the other Seller Parties to) cooperate therewith, at the Purchaser's expense. The Purchaser hereby requests, and the Seller hereby grants (and shall cause the other Seller Parties to grant) to the Purchaser and its Affiliates, all rights necessary to record this IP Agreement or such documentation with the United States Patent and Trademark Office, the United States Copyright Office, and any equivalent office or agency in any jurisdiction in the world. Seller shall (and shall cause the other Seller Parties) to, between the Effective Date and the Initial Closing Date, cooperate with the Purchaser in connection with the Purchaser's preparation for acquiring the Business, including (i) by reasonably sharing information to prevent any loss of any of the Seller Entities' rights to any Intellectual Property constituting Transferred IP or Licensed IP, and (ii) facilitating between the parties and their respective Affiliates' communication and sharing of information related to this IP Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The modified Section 2.3 introduces significant contradictions with Section 6.5. The original Section 2.3 mandates the Seller to provide 'complete and accurate copies' of documentation promptly. The changed text weakens this obligation by changing 'shall provide' to 'may provide', altering the timing to a discretionary period of 10 years, reducing the scope of delivery, and indicating that the provided copies could be 'incomplete and potentially inaccurate'. This directly conflicts with Section 6.5, which requires the Seller to 'execute and deliver such other documents and take such other actions as the other party may reasonably request to effectuate the transactions'. By creating uncertainty around the completeness and accuracy of documentation and introducing a discretionary timeline, the modified Section 2.3 directly undermines the Seller's obligation in Section 6.5 to cooperate in perfecting rights in the Transferred IP and sharing information necessary for the Purchaser to acquire the Business.
